labs.clinicalContext: Combination RAS blockade increases risk of hyperkalemia

labs.actionRequired: Monitor for acute kidney injury especially with combination therapy
labs.clinicalContext: Combination RAS blockade increases risk of acute kidney injury
Monitor kidney function as combination RAS blockade associated with higher AKI rates
RAS inhibitors reduce albuminuria but monitoring needed to assess effectiveness

labRequirements.specialPopulationNotes: Adults with CKD (G1-G5, G5D) and diabetes (T1D and T2D). Avoid combination RAS blockade due to increased hyperkalemia and AKI risk.
labRequirements.sourceSnippet: Long-term outcome trials in patients with diabetes and CKD demonstrated no kidney or cardiovascular benefit of RAS blockade with combined therapy to block the RAS versus the single use of RAS inhibitors. However, combination therapy was associated with a higher rate of hyperkalemia and AKI, and thus only one agent at a time should be used to block the RAS.

governance.sourceDocument: KDIGO 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline for Diabetes Management in Chronic Kidney Disease
